# Plot life expectancy vs population

# Fit a linear model 
# linear model life_expectancy vs infant_mortality 
fit1 <- lm(life_expectancy ~ infant_mortality, data = af_2000)

# display summary about fit1

# linear model life_expectancy vs population

Multiple R-squared:  0.005176,  Adjusted R-squared:  -0.01513

From the result in fit1, in 2000, the infant mortality rate in African countries was significantly negatively correlated with life expectancy (p < 0.001). For every 1 unit increase in the infant mortality rate, the life expectancy was expected to decrease by approximately 0.19 years. From the result in fit2, in 2000, there was no significant correlation between the population size of African countries and their life expectancy (p = 0.616). Changes in population size were unable to effectively predict the level of life expectancy in these countries. In contrast, infant mortality rate was a more crucial predictive factor.

title: Data Exercise(Week4)
text:
In this section, I generate a synthetic dataset (N=1000) representing an older adult population (50+). The underlying logic includes:


Age & Depression: Strong risk factors for dementia.
Education & Physical Activity: Protective factors.
Comorbidities (Diabetes/Hypertension): Driven by Age and BMI.
BMI & Dementia: A U-shaped relationship(both underweight and obese increase risk).


# 0) Load required packages
library(tidyverse)

тФАтФА Attaching core tidyverse packages тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА tidyverse 2.0.0 тФАтФА
тЬФ dplyr     1.2.0     тЬФ readr     2.2.0
тЬФ forcats   1.0.1     тЬФ stringr   1.6.0
тЬФ ggplot2   4.0.2     тЬФ tibble    3.3.1
тЬФ lubridate 1.9.5     тЬФ tidyr     1.3.2
тЬФ purrr     1.2.1     
тФАтФА Conflicts тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА tidyverse_conflicts() тФАтФА
тЬЦ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
тЬЦ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
тД╣ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(ggplot2)
# 1) Reproducibility seed
base::set.seed(1)

# 2) functions
inv_logit <- function(x) 1 / (1 + base::exp(-x))
clamp <- function(x, lower, upper) base::pmax(lower, base::pmin(x, upper))

# 3) Sample size
N <- 1000

# 4) Generate synthetic dataset
synthetic_data <- tibble::tibble(
  ID = base::sprintf("%05d", 1:N),

  # Age: 85% from truncated normal-like around 65, 15% from uniform tails
  age = {
    mix <- stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = 0.85)
    main <- clamp(stats::rnorm(n = N, mean = 65, sd = 7.5), 50, 95)
    tail <- stats::runif(n = N, min = 50, max = 95)
    base::as.integer(base::round(base::ifelse(mix == 1, main, tail)))
  },

  # Gender: ~52% female
  gender = {
    g <- stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = 0.52)
    base::factor(base::ifelse(g == 1, "Female", "Male"), levels = c("Male", "Female"))
  }
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Education (years): mean ~13 with mild cohort effect (older -> slightly fewer years)
    education_years = {
      edu_base <- stats::rnorm(n = N, mean = 13, sd = 2.7)
      edu <- clamp(edu_base - 0.03 * (age - 65), 0, 22)
      base::as.integer(base::round(edu))
    },

    # BMI: mean ~27 sd ~5, mild age pattern, clamped
    BMI = {
      bmi_base <- stats::rnorm(n = N, mean = 27, sd = 5)
      bmi_val <- clamp(bmi_base + 0.03 * (pmin(age, 70) - 50), 15, 55)
      base::round(bmi_val, 1)
    },

    # Physical activity: ordered factor influenced by education (+) and age (-)
    physical_activity = {
      latent <- 0.12 * (education_years - 12) - 0.03 * (age - 65) + stats::rnorm(n = N)
      pa <- base::cut(
        latent,
        breaks = c(-Inf, -0.4, 0.8, Inf),
        labels = c("Low", "Moderate", "High"),
        right = TRUE
      )
      base::factor(pa, levels = c("Low", "Moderate", "High"), ordered = TRUE)
    },

    # Depression history: ~15тАУ20% with links to age and physical activity
    depression_history = {
      lp <- -1.6 +
        0.012 * (age - 65) +
        0.25  * (physical_activity == "Low") -
        0.10  * (physical_activity == "High") +
        0.08  * (gender == "Female")
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    },

    # Diabetes and hypertension: probabilities increase with age and BMI
    diabetes = {
      lp <- -2.0 + 0.035 * (age - 65) + 0.070 * (BMI - 27) + 0.15 * (gender == "Male")
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    },

    hypertension = {
      lp <- -1.0 + 0.050 * (age - 65) + 0.040 * (BMI - 27) + 0.10 * (gender == "Male")
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    },

    # Number of comorbidities (0,1,2) among diabetes + hypertension
    n_comorbidities = diabetes + hypertension,

    # Dementia (0/1): age & depression strong risk; edu & PA protective;
    # diabetes & hypertension risk; BMI U-shaped via quadratic term
    dementia = {
      bmi_u <- ((BMI - 27)^2) / 25
      lp <- -3.2 +
        0.075 * (age - 65) +
        0.85  * depression_history +
        (-0.08) * (education_years - 13) +
        (-0.25) * (physical_activity == "Moderate") +
        (-0.50) * (physical_activity == "High") +
        0.45  * diabetes +
        0.35  * hypertension +
        0.55  * bmi_u
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    }
  )

# 3) Sample size

  ID = base::sprintf("%05d", 1:N),

    tail <- stats::runif(n = N, min = 50, max = 95)
    base::as.integer(base::round(base::ifelse(mix == 1, main, tail)))

    # Education (years): mean ~13 with mild cohort effect (older -> slightly fewer years)
    education_years = {
      edu_base <- stats::rnorm(n = N, mean = 13, sd = 2.7)
      edu <- clamp(edu_base - 0.03 * (age - 65), 0, 22)
      base::as.integer(base::round(edu))
    },

    # BMI: mean ~27 sd ~5, mild age pattern, clamped
    BMI = {
      bmi_base <- stats::rnorm(n = N, mean = 27, sd = 5)
      bmi_val <- clamp(bmi_base + 0.03 * (pmin(age, 70) - 50), 15, 55)
      base::round(bmi_val, 1)
    },

    # Physical activity: ordered factor influenced by education (+) and age (-)
    physical_activity = {
      latent <- 0.12 * (education_years - 12) - 0.03 * (age - 65) + stats::rnorm(n = N)
      pa <- base::cut(
        latent,
        breaks = c(-Inf, -0.4, 0.8, Inf),
        labels = c("Low", "Moderate", "High"),
        right = TRUE
      )
      base::factor(pa, levels = c("Low", "Moderate", "High"), ordered = TRUE)
    },

    # Depression history: ~15тАУ20% with links to age and physical activity
    depression_history = {
      lp <- -1.6 +
        0.012 * (age - 65) +
        0.25  * (physical_activity == "Low") -
        0.10  * (physical_activity == "High") +
        0.08  * (gender == "Female")
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    },

    # Diabetes and hypertension: probabilities increase with age and BMI
    diabetes = {
      lp <- -2.0 + 0.035 * (age - 65) + 0.070 * (BMI - 27) + 0.15 * (gender == "Male")
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    },

    hypertension = {
      lp <- -1.0 + 0.050 * (age - 65) + 0.040 * (BMI - 27) + 0.10 * (gender == "Male")
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    },

    # Number of comorbidities (0,1,2) among diabetes + hypertension
    n_comorbidities = diabetes + hypertension,

    # Dementia (0/1): age & depression strong risk; edu & PA protective;
    # diabetes & hypertension risk; BMI U-shaped via quadratic term
    dementia = {
      bmi_u <- ((BMI - 27)^2) / 25
      lp <- -3.2 +
        0.075 * (age - 65) +
        0.85  * depression_history +
        (-0.08) * (education_years - 13) +
        (-0.25) * (physical_activity == "Moderate") +
        (-0.50) * (physical_activity == "High") +
        0.45  * diabetes +
        0.35  * hypertension +
        0.55  * bmi_u
      stats::rbinom(n = N, size = 1, prob = inv_logit(lp))
    }
  )

  geom_smooth(method = "loess") +
  labs(title = "U-shaped BMI relationship with dementia risk", x = "BMI", y = "Probability (loess-smoothed)")

title: About me
text:
Background and Training
I am a first-year PhD student in Health Services Research, with a background in statistics. My academic training has provided me with experience in quantitative methods and data analysis, and I am particularly interested in applying these tools to real-world public health and policy questions.


Research Interests
My research interests focus on public health, healthcare access, and health policy. I am especially interested in understanding how social, economic, and policy environments shape health outcomes and contribute to disparities across populations.


Data Analysis and Programming Experience
I have previously used R and Python for data analysis and visualization. While I am still building confidence and fluency in these tools, I am comfortable working with data and eager to continue improving my programming and analytical skills.


Goals for This Course
Through this course, I hope to strengthen my ability to conduct reproducible data analyses, gain more experience with modern data science tools, and further improve my proficiency in R for applied data analysis.


A Dataset I Have Been Working With
One dataset I have recently been working with is the Health and Retirement Study (HRS), a nationally representative longitudinal survey of older adults in the United States.
I have been using HRS in recent research related to dementia, which has made me particularly familiar with the structure and variables of the dataset. I find HRS especially valuable because it provides rich longitudinal information on health, socioeconomic status, and aging, allowing for applied statistical analyses that are directly relevant to public health and health policy questions.
More information about the dataset can be found here:
https://hrs.isr.umich.edu

Summary
The original CDC dataset shows real-world structure and patterns. Some states (like Texas) have many more counties than others, population is strongly right-skewed, and health measures such as obesity and diabetes cluster around realistic averages. There is also a clear positive relationship between obesity and diabetes, which reflects known public health trends.
In contrast, the synthetic dataset was generated randomly. States are more evenly distributed, population does not show strong skewness, and health measures are spread evenly across chosen ranges. Because the variables were simulated independently, there is no meaningful relationship between obesity and diabetes in the synthetic data.
Overall, the synthetic data matches the structure (same columns and number of rows) but does not reproduce the real-world patterns seen in the original CDC dataset.

# keep one OOC = 1
data_OOC1 <- data_raw %>%
  filter(OCC == 1)

# Create a new data frame with the sum of DV for each individual, excluding time 0
y_dt <- data_OOC1 %>%
  filter(TIME != 0) %>%
  group_by(ID) %>%
  summarize(Y = sum(DV, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# keep time 0 data
time0 <- data_OOC1 %>%
  filter(TIME == 0) 

# Merge the two data frames to include the sum of DV and the time 0 data
d1 <- time0 %>%
  left_join(y_dt, by = "ID")

# Converts RACE and SEX to factor variables and keeps only these variables: Y,DOSE,AGE,SEX,RACE,WT,HT
d1 <- d1 %>%
  mutate(
    RACE = as.factor(RACE),
    SEX = as.factor(SEX),
    DOSE = as.factor(DOSE)

  select(Y, DOSE, AGE, SEX, WT, HT) # RACE removed

# Reset seed before CV 
set.seed(rngseed)

# Create 10-fold CV folds from training data
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10)

# Null model CV spec

  set_engine("parsnip") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

cv_null <- fit_resamples(
  null_spec,
  Y ~ .,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

# CV for Model 1
cv_dose <- fit_resamples(
  lm_spec,
  Y ~ DOSE,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

# CV for Model 2
cv_all <- fit_resamples(
  lm_spec,
  Y ~ .,
  resamples = cv_folds,

)

The linear model using DOSE alone reduces the RMSE to around 702, indicating a substantial improvement in predictive accuracy. Adding all predictors further reduces the RMSE to about 627, demonstrating that these additional variables contribute to better explaining the variability in Y. ### Cross-validation for null model, model 1, and model 2

set.seed(rngseed)

# Create 10-fold CV folds from training data

# Null model CV spec

  set_engine("parsnip") %>%
  set_mode("regression")

cv_null <- fit_resamples(
  null_spec,
  Y ~ .,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

# CV for Model 1
cv_dose <- fit_resamples(
  lm_spec,
  Y ~ DOSE,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

# CV for Model 2
cv_all <- fit_resamples(
  lm_spec,
  Y ~ .,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

# Compare results
collect_metrics(cv_null)

The CV RMSE values are 933 (null), 697 (Model 1), and 653 (Model 2). Compared to the training set RMSE (948, 703, 627), the null model and Model 1 remain similar, while Model 2тАЩs CV RMSE is slightly higher than its training RMSE (653 vs 627), suggesting mild overfitting. text:

# 0) Load required packages
library(tidyverse)

тФАтФА Attaching core tidyverse packages тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА tidyverse 2.0.0 тФАтФА

тЬФ forcats   1.0.1     тЬФ stringr   1.6.0
тЬФ ggplot2   4.0.1     тЬФ tibble    3.3.1
тЬФ lubridate 1.9.5     тЬФ tidyr     1.3.2
тЬФ purrr     1.2.1     
тФАтФА Conflicts тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА tidyverse_conflicts() тФАтФА

library(ggplot2)
library(here)

# 1) Read the FiveThirtyEight/NCAA tournament history dataset
raw <- readr::read_csv(
  here("presentation-exercise", "ncaa-womens-basketball-tournament-history.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# 2) Keep only the four programs shown in the original figure
#    (Note: in the dataset, Long Beach State is labeled as "Long Beach St.")
schools_keep <- c(
  "Louisiana Tech",
  "Long Beach St.",
  "Southern California",
  "Old Dominion"
)

# 3) Build the plotting dataset with the required columns:
#    year (1982тАУ2018), school, seed_points (0тАУ100)
plot_df <- raw %>%
  # Limit to the target time window and selected schools
  filter(Year >= 1982, Year <= 2018, School %in% schools_keep) %>%
  # Keep only what we need and standardize names
  transmute(
    year   = Year,
    school = School,
    seed   = as.numeric(Seed)
  ) %>%
  # Convert NCAA seed (1 is best, 16 is worst) into a 0тАУ100 "seed points" scale
  # Higher seed_points = better seed / stronger tournament position
  mutate(seed_points = (17 - seed) / 16 * 100) %>%
  select(year, school, seed_points) %>%
  # Fill in missing years for each school (years without a tournament appearance)
  # so the line/area sits at 0, matching the look of the FiveThirtyEight figure.
  complete(
    year = 1982:2018,
    school = schools_keep,
    fill = list(seed_points = 0)
  ) %>%
  # Clean up the displayed school name and set facet order
  mutate(
    school = recode(school, "Long Beach St." = "Long Beach State"),
    school = factor(
      school,
      levels = c(
        "Louisiana Tech",
        "Long Beach State",
        "Southern California",
        "Old Dominion"
      )
    )
  )

# 4) Colors chosen to resemble the original FiveThirtyEight palette
cols <- c(
  "Louisiana Tech"      = "#4FA3D9",
  "Long Beach State"    = "#F2C230",
  "Southern California" = "#F28C6B",
  "Old Dominion"        = "#9E9E9E"
)

# 5) Recreate the chart: area + line, faceted into a 2x2 grid
p <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = year, y = seed_points, color = school, fill = school)) +
  # Lightly filled area under the line
  geom_area(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0) +
  # Outline line on top of the area
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  # Four small multiples (2 columns -> 2x2 layout)
  facet_wrap(~ school, ncol = 2) +
  # Apply manual colors for both line and fill
  scale_color_manual(values = cols) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = cols) +
  # Match the 0тАУ100 y-axis scale and tick spacing
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 100, 20)) +
  # Match the x-axis labeling style (with abbreviated years)
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(1985, 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015),
    labels = c("1985", "'90", "'95", "2000", "'05", "'10", "'15")
  ) +
  # Titles and source caption
  labs(
    title = "The fall of yesterdayтАЩs womenтАЩs basketball powerhouses",
    subtitle = "Seed points by year for selected womenтАЩs college programs, 1982тАУ2018",
    caption = "SOURCE: NCAA",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  ) +
  # FiveThirtyEight-style minimal look
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#D9D9D9", linewidth = 0.6),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#4D4D4D"),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "#8C8C8C"),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "#8C8C8C")
  )
p

title: presentation-exercise (Week6)
text:
Prompt
Write R code to create a professional summary table from the NCAA basketball dataset that includes automatically calculated statistics, bold text, conditional color formatting, a spanner header, a footnote, a caption, and inline sparklines.

# Load table formatting packages
library(gt)
library(gtExtras)

# 1) Process data: Calculate summary statistics for the top 5 schools (by total wins)
# and create a list column of their historical win trends for the sparkline.
top_schools_summary <- raw %>%
  # Filter for recent history to make sparklines legible (e.g., 2000 onwards)
  filter(Year >= 2000) %>%
  mutate(
    tourney_w = as.numeric(`Tourney W`),
    seed = as.numeric(Seed)
  ) %>%
  # Remove NA rows that might break calculations
  filter(!is.na(tourney_w), !is.na(seed)) %>%
  group_by(School) %>%
  summarize(
    Appearances = n(),
    Total_Wins = sum(tourney_w),
    Avg_Wins_per_Tourney = Total_Wins / Appearances,
    Avg_Seed = mean(seed),
    # Create a list of wins ordered by year for the sparkline
    Wins_History = list(tourney_w[order(Year)]),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  # Keep only the top 5 schools with the most wins since 2000
  slice_max(order_by = Total_Wins, n = 5)
top_schools_summary <- top_schools_summary %>%
  mutate(School = stringr::str_replace_all(School, " ", "\u00A0"))

# 2) Build the gt table
top_schools_table <- top_schools_summary %>%
  gt() %>%
